exemplar.title: How do different volume concentrations of ethanol impact the refractive index of a water-ethanol solution?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do different volume concentrations of ethanol impact the refractive index of a water-ethanol solution?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording and processing are communicated clearly through well-labelled raw and processed tables, units, and a plotted graph.
The apparatus list includes uncertainty specifications, enhancing transparency.
Data processing steps (degreeâminute conversion, averaging) are described in a clear and systematic manner.
weaknesses: Formatting glitches (mis-typed minutes, blank columns) and undefined placeholders reduce precision in data presentation.
Uncertainties are only partially considered; propagation into refractive indices is omitted and systematic error sources (container thickness, dispersion) are not quantified.
Calculation inconsistencies (e.g., incorrect degreeâminute conversion, implausibly high n-value for pure ethanol) indicate processing inaccuracies.
Error analysis uses placeholder symbols and lacks full formulas, leading to significant omissions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing is âclear and preciseâ overall, fitting the 5-6 band, but formatting errors and placeholders limit precision slightlyâjustifying a band-4 allocation. Consideration of uncertainties shows some treatment (resolution propagation) but significant omissions in propagation to refractive indices, placing it in band 3-4. Data processing exhibits inconsistencies and inaccuracies, aligning with band 3-4. Balancing these factors, a score of 4 reflects strong clarity tempered by notable omissions and errors.

strengths: The research question is sharply defined and situated within a specific context linking refractive index relevance to optics and renewable fuels.
Relevant theoretical frameworks (Snellâs law) and specific boundary conditions (constant temperature and pressure) are cited to justify the investigationâs importance.
The apparatus list and step-by-step method are presented logically, enabling reproducibility with minimal ambiguities.
A clear, testable hypothesis predicting a linear relationship between ethanol concentration and refractive index is articulated.
weaknesses: Some literature references are incomplete, reducing academic rigour.
The table following Snellâs law omits the key equation, hindering reproducibility.
Units for ethanol concentration switch between g/mL and percentages, introducing ambiguity.
The alignment procedure for the laser and phone app lacks detailed, step-by-step instructions.
One method step ends mid-sentence, leaving the incidence-angle rationale unfinished.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work solidly in the 5-6 band: the research question is contextualized specifically, theory is cited, and boundary conditions are clear (band 5-6 descriptor). The methodological description allows reproduction with only minor clarifications needed (band 3-4 top), and methodological considerations are described though not fully explained quantitatively (band 3-4). Minor citation gaps and incomplete procedural details prevent a full 6, making a score of 5 the most appropriate to reflect high quality with slight omissions.

strengths: The conclusion directly links the observed linear trend and slope back to the hypothesis and research question, ensuring consistency with analysis.
The best-fit line behaviour is described clearly, highlighting deviations and trend reliability.
A definitive statement that the research question has been answered provides closure.
weaknesses: No numerical comparison to accepted literature values for refractive index is made, limiting the depth of justification.
The discussion of anomalies in the data is superficial rather than analytical.
The best-fit equation is presented without clear units or uncertainty notation.
Theoretical context (e.g., Lorentz-Lorenz or Gladstone-Dale relations) is not invoked to strengthen the scientific comparison.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is justified and consistent with the analysis (band 5-6 descriptor) but lacks quantitative comparison to standard values and deeper theoretical context, placing it in the 3-4 band. The superficial treatment of anomalies further constrains the score. A band-4 score balances the clear, relevant conclusion with these areas for improvement.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses are identified (e.g., beaker resolution, alignment drift, temperature variation) and linked to corresponding error types.
Realistic and relevant improvements are proposed (e.g., higher-precision volumetric ware, AI-based angle measurement, conducting experiments in a single day).
Each suggested improvement is accompanied by an explanation of how it would reduce error, demonstrating thoughtful planning.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness is not quantitatively ranked or compared.
Improvement suggestions lack numerical estimates of expected benefit or required resources.
A timeline or resource requirement for implementing each suggestion is not provided.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation articulates specific methodological weaknesses and proposes realistic, relevant improvements with clear rationales, satisfying the 5-6 descriptor for suggested improvements. However, it stops short of quantitatively assessing the relative impact of each weakness or improvement (band 3-4 for methodological weaknesses). On balance, a score of 5 accurately reflects thorough evaluation with minor omissions in quantitative impact analysis.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states a focused question (âHow do different volume concentrations of ethanol impact the refractive indexâ¦â) and immediately situates it within the practical contexts of optics, renewable fuels and previous concentration studies.  Relevant theory (Snellâs law, external literature) and specific boundary conditions (constant temperature / pressure) are cited, showing the reader exactly why the relationship is worth probing.  Minor weaknessesâe.g., a largely personal anecdote and some citation gapsâprevent a perfect 6, but the context remains specific and appropriate, comfortably meeting the top-band descriptor.

Instrument resolution is quoted (Â±0.005â², Â±0.01Â°) and propagated to a slope uncertainty, showing some treatment of error.  Nevertheless, uncertainties are not propagated into the calculated refractive indices, nor are systematic sources (container thickness, wavelength dispersion) quantified.  The studentâs error analysis therefore contains âsignificant omissionsâ, matching the 3-4 band but leaning toward its lower end.

Key variables, controls and uncontrolled factors are listed and briefly described, and the student recognises evaporation and alignment drift as possible issues.  However, explanations of *why* each control matters (e.g., temperature dependence of RI) are cursory, and no quantitative justification for the chosen incidence angle or volume steps is given.  Hence the considerations are âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplainedâ, meriting the middle of the 3-4 band.

Specific weaknessesâbeaker resolution, app alignment, incidence-angle drift, temperature variationâare each linked to the type of error and the potential magnitude of impact, moving beyond mere listing.  The narrative stops short of evaluating *relative* impact quantitatively (e.g., error propagation estimates), so the strand is best described as âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplainedâ, placing it in 3-4 with the higher score for its detail.

The apparatus list, step-wise method, and photo of the set-up would allow another student to reproduce the experiment with only a few clarifications (exact path length of the laser, container dimensions, how the angle-measuring app is positioned).  Eleven concentrations, five repeats and a fixed incidence angle are specified, satisfying âfew ambiguities or omissionsâ.  Because the alignment procedure for the phone app and the exact Snell calculation are not fully spelled out, reproduction is not wholly seamless, aligning with the 3-4 band ceiling.

Averages are correctly computed and Snellâs law is applied to obtain n-values, producing a sensible monotonic trend.  However, several conversion slips (e.g., 25Â° 47â² written as 25.58Â°) and an implausibly high value of n=1.58 for pure ethanol indicate inaccuracies.  Because these are not isolated typographical errors but influence the processed data, the work fits âsome significant omissions or inconsistenciesâ rather than the top band.

For each cited weakness the student proposes a realistic improvement and explains how it would reduce error (e.g., lower-uncertainty volumetric ware to cut systematic error, AI-based angle measurement to minimise human alignment bias, conducting experiment in one day to stabilise temperature).  The rationale behind each recommendation is explicit, though not always quantitatively justified; this thoroughness satisfies the descriptor for the top band, with a small deduction from the maximum for lacking numerical evaluation of expected benefit.

Raw-data and processed-data tables are clearly labelled; units and uncertainties are generally present, and a graph is provided.  Formatting glitches (mis-typed minutes, blank table columns) and the undefined â¡ placeholders reduce precision, but the overall communication remains clear enough for the reader to follow the analysis pathway, fulfilling the âclear *and* preciseâ requirement reasonably well.

Early sections reference literature showing concentration-dependent RI, and the conclusion briefly notes that the obtained linearity is âsimilarâ to sugar-solution studies.  However, there is no numerical comparison to standard n-values for ethanol-water mixtures, nor is theory (Lorentz-Lorenz, Gladstone-Dale) invoked.  Thus the comparison is present but only âsomewhat relevantâ, matching the mid-band.

The student links the linear trend, equation and slope to the hypothesis and explicitly states that the research question is answered, demonstrating consistency with the analysis.  Still, the discussion of anomalies is superficial and no quantitative comparison with accepted n-values is made here, limiting the justification depth and placing the work solidly in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: Future improvements heading is present but could be enhanced with a timeline or resource requirements for each suggestion.
Controlled variables are stated but lack quantitative justification (e.g. why a 35Â° incidence angle was chosen). Explain rationale numerically.
Apparatus list is comprehensive and includes uncertainty specs, facilitating reproducibility.
Method step 3 ends mid-sentence, leaving the incidence angle rationale incomplete. Ensure each method step is fully articulated.
All method steps are presented in logical order, aiding clarity for replication.
Safety considerations are thorough, addressing chemical, glassware, and laser hazards, which enhances ethical compliance.
Data collection description refers to Figure 3 but omits explicit raw data table placement. Cite table numbers when mentioning collected data.
The method for converting 25Â° 47â² to decimal degrees mistakenly uses 35â² rather than 47â². Correct conversion: $$25+\frac{47}{60}=25.78^\circ$$.
Equation (5) shows conversion steps but contains a typographical error in the second line. Verify each minute fraction carefully to avoid propagation errors.
Data processing description is clear, detailing conversion from degreesâminutes to decimal form and averaging procedures.
Average angle table (Figure 4) omits uncertainty propagation into refractive index values. Include error bars for each computed n-value.
The table header uses colspan incorrectly, leading to misalignment of uncertainty label. Adjust table structure for consistency.
Final refractive index table includes an implausibly high value for 100% ethanol (n=1.581), suggesting calculation or unit conversion errors. Review Snellâs law application.

The research question is clearly stated and situated within a specific context, linking refractive index relevance to optics and renewable fuels. This enhances the investigationâs focus and relevance.
Background discussion cites Snellâs law and material applications, but some literature references lack complete details. Adding full citations will strengthen academic rigour.
The definition of refractive index is described, but the equation formatting uses placeholder images instead of LaTeX. Replace image placeholders with $$n=\frac{c}{v}$$ for clarity.

Table 8 contains generic âSquareâ placeholders instead of the actual Snellâs law equation. Including the correct formula $$n=\frac{\sin\theta_i}{\sin\theta_r}$$ will improve methodological transparency.
The table following Snellâs law (Table 11) lacks the equation content entirely. Ensure the refraction relationship $$n_1\sin\theta_1=n_2\sin\theta_2$$ is displayed for reproducibility.
The decision to hold temperature and pressure constant is well justified, demonstrating clear methodological control of external variables.
The literature review mentions sugar-solution refractive index trends but lacks quantitative comparison to ethanolâwater data. Including specific values will deepen the context.
Hypothesis is clearly stated and testable, predicting a linear relationship between ethanol concentration and refractive index.
Variables table lists ethanol concentration in g/mL but then refers to percentages. Clarify units consistently to avoid ambiguity during data collection.
The description of the setup header is present but the alignment procedure for the laser and phone app is not detailed. Add step-by-step alignment instructions.
Figure 3 raw data table uses inconsistent angle notation (e.g., 25Â° 43â² vs. 2548â²), reducing clarity. Adopt a uniform format.
Evaluation table describes weaknesses but omits discussion of their relative impact magnitude. Rank errors by expected effect size.
Presentation of n-values is systematic and facilitates trend recognition across concentrations.
Conclusion accurately describes linear trend but lacks numerical comparison to standard literature values for refractive index, limiting depth of justification.
The best-fit equation is reported without clear units or coefficient context. Format as $$n=(2.288\pm0.002)\times10^{-3}[\%]+1.299$$ for precision.
Description of best-fit line behaviour (points above/below) is clear and aids understanding of data deviations.
Uncertainty calculation uses placeholder symbols and unclear formulas. Replace boxes with proper slope limits and express $$\Delta m=\frac{m_{max}-m_{min}}{2}$$.
Error percentage presentation lacks context on how it influences final refractive index accuracy. Discuss how this 0.1% uncertainty affects conclusions.
Final claim clearly restates that the research question is answered, providing closure to the investigation.
Evaluation table lists specific weaknesses and links them to error types, demonstrating good analysis of methodological limitations.
Recommendation to use AI-based angle measurement is innovative and directly addresses human-alignment errors.
Suggestion to complete the experiment in one day is realistic; however, quantify how temperature drift over hours affects refractive index changes.
Reference list includes diverse sources but citation style is inconsistent. Use a single style (APA or MLA) throughout.
Yunus & Rahman is cited in-text but missing page or section detail. Add precise locator to strengthen credibility.
Additional references cover relevant refractive index literature, demonstrating good breadth of background research.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
